    IFMIS users, an integrated helpdesk solution has been installed at the Ministry of Finance and associated systems
    EDMS and EFT have had business process requirements completed. The Chart of Accounts was also upgraded from
    27 to 33 digits. Although there was no associated PDO level indicator, there was an intermediate indicator “MDAs
    processing transactions real time from IFMIS at rolled-out sites” which was 55 by the end of the project, with 6
    MDAs experiencing technical issues that were preventing them from processing transactions real time which
    exceeded the initial target of 54 MDAs.

38. Enhancements to financial systems have likely contributed to improvements independently validated in other
    important areas of the budget cycle. Although it is difficult to make specific connections between the
    enhancements to the financial systems noted above and some key budget cycle performance indicators, it is likely
    that improved systems facilitated improvements that flow from systems enhancement that facilitate expenditure
    management which have been noted by PEFA assessments in the areas of “expenditure arrears”, “payroll controls”,
    and “financial data integrity”. All these indicators showed improvements between the 2018 PEFA assessment and
    the 2021 draft PEFA assessment. All transactions recorded in IFMIS result in an audit trail and access to the system
    has now got strengthened access controls which has led to some improvements in payroll controls and financial
    data integrity. Expenditure arrears management is now supported by improved data from the system and used to
    create a comprehensive database to track and monitor arrears.

39. Whilst an end-to-end e-Procurement system was unable to be achieved as planned under the project import
    groundwork for its eventual implementation has been made helping to make the public procurement system
    more effective, efficient, and transparent. An e-GP directorate has been created and the secretariat for electronic
    contract management was moved within this new directorate. An e-GP action plan is in place. While the
    Procurement Act already provides for e-Procurement, Public Procurement Regulations were updated in late 2019 to
    ensure legal coverage and mandating of the electronic procurement in GoSL’s legal framework. A cost benefit
    analysis for hosting the e-GP system has been complete which provides important information for the e-GP bidding
    document. The e-GP functional specifications have also been completed and a bidding document for the e-GP
    system has been completed. Finally, an e-GP awareness raising campaign has been achieved with 682 participants
    targeted in all four regions.

40. Procurement price norms established since 2019 have led to significant savings and more competitive
    procurement is happening. In terms of general procurement, legal amendments were made to the Procurement
    Act to codify the usage of price norms as a policy and Government has assessed that this provided savings of US
    $7.7m between Q1, 2019 and Q3, 2021. A public procurement capacity building strategy and curriculum have also
    been completed and training was provided to over 100 public procurement practitioners. The preparation of
    standard bidding documents for specialized procurements (health, ICT and management services) and the
    development of a contract management database with security and audit trail features has helped to reduce the
    amount of non-competitive, as indicated below, and helped with contract management- although this latter area
    continues to be an area for significant improvement.

41. Despite not operationalizing an e-Procurement system, the PDO indicator was close to achievement and the
    related intermediate indicators were achieved. The PDO level indicator in procurement was “the percentage of
    contracts (by value) awarded in the fiscal year through open procurement”, which from a baseline of 0% was
    targeting 95% and was not achieved at 91% by the end of the project. The last ISR reported that the percentage of
    contracts (by value) awarded in the fiscal year through open competitive procurement was 91% with a target by the


                                                                                                             Page 22 of 65
        The World Bank
       Public Financial Management Improvement and Consolidation Project (P133424)



    end of the project of 95%. It was noted that the ISR prior had achieved 94%, but with the advent of COVID-19 there
    were more sole-sourced contracts awarded due to the emergency nature of the expenditures during the pandemic.
    With a baseline of 0%, the increase is nevertheless very significant. The intermediate indicators for procurement
    were “largest MDA budget heads with annual procurement plans within annual budget ceiling” and “% of public
    procurement professionals within the Government of Sierra Leone trained on e-procurement”, these were both
    exceeded.

42. Improvements in procurement practices were evidenced by the 2021 draft PEFA assessment, however, there is
    still work to be done. The draft PEFA report for 2021 reflected a positive change to procurement with a rating of
    “C” compared with the 2018 comparative of “D”. The reasons behind the noted improvement include the
    establishment of an independent procurement review panel, publication of all MDA procurement plans and the
    publication of data on the resolution of procurement complaints. It is also noted that there is ongoing work under
    DPF operations to support the publication of contract awards and implementation of OCDS as a precursor for e-
    procurement.

43. Cash management through Treasury Single Accounts (TSA), Public Investment Management (PIM) and Local
    Council budgeting and accountability received relatively minor project support prior to MTR and were dropped
    following the restructuring of the project in 2017. As of 2021 16 MDAs are within the TSA system, and this is
    helping cash management, while the project did not play a significant role in this progress, it did provide some initial
    training and support. For PIM, the project supported the establishment of the Public Investment Management
    Division with equipment and training and the development of the Public Investment Operation Manuel which is
    called for by the PFM Regulations 2018. For Local Councils, support was provided to build capacity for planning and
    budgeting with a framework for budgeting and reporting developed and sensitized, and support on training for the
    PETRA accounting system and subsequently a needs assessment for the implementation of the PFM Smart
    accounting system. Each of these PFM areas have made little progress over the duration of the project in terms of
    outcomes; the local councils will receive support under the new Accountable Governance for Basic Service Delivery
    project and pick-up from where this project left off.

    Outcome (ii): Improving accountability in the use of government finances

44. The approach taken to improve the accountability in the use of government finances was to strengthen capacity
    to implement and enforce the oversight reforms contained in the PFM Act 2016 and the PFM Regulations 2018
    and increase citizen engagement in holding Government accountable for the use of public finances. The project
    supported capacity building of internal auditors and supported the establishment of audit committees15 at 15 MDAs
    to provide independence to internal auditors and follow-up on the implementation of internal audit
    recommendations. Support was provided to the Audit Service Sierra Leone in undertaking performance audits. A
    Public Accounts Committee Secretariat was established with the support of the project to improve the quality of
    legislative oversight. The project continued to support a Non-state Actors (NSA) Secretariat established under the
    predecessor project (IPFMRP) to continue with providing grants to NSAs to enhance citizen engagement in the
    management of public finances, and in order to increase access to information.


         15Audit Committees are constituted under the PFM Act 2016 and are to comprise 3-5 members with an external Chair and
         another external with accounting and auditing experience. The audit committees help the internal audit teams at the MDAs be
         more independent of the Executive and they are charged to discuss the external and internal audit issues raised.

                                                                                                                               Page 23 of 65
        The World Bank
       Public Financial Management Improvement and Consolidation Project (P133424)



45. Important progress was made in capacity to oversee internal controls and risks at MDAs through the internal
    audit function and the establishment of audit committees, but this did not translate to improvements in the
    control environments of MDAs. Initially, internal audit effectiveness (PEFA assessment) was originally a PDO
    indicator but after the shift of the projects focus after the MTR to the rollout of PFM systems, the indicator was
    replaced with two intermediate indicators including the coverage of internal audit (as a percentage of total
    expenditures) and the percentage of internal audit recommendations implemented. By the project’s closure, the
    internal audit function was established in 45 MDAs and covered over 85% of expenditure which exceeded the target
    of 65%, and the project supported the Internal Audit Directorate’s expansion with training and professional
    development. However, the percentage of internal audit recommendations that were implemented by the end of
    the project was well below the 50% target at 29%. It is challenging to assess whether the issue with implementation
    regards the work of internal auditors and their understanding of the issues including the way issues are
    communicated and the actions called or whether the problem is with the MDAs, most likely it is a combination of
    these factors and work will be done under an ASA subsequently to support Government on this.

46. While 25 audit committees have been constituted, they have not been effective in supporting the resolution of
    internal control issues reported by the internal audit function. There are an inadequate number of functioning
    audit committees at MDAs. That gap between internal audit coverage (45 MDAs) and audit committee coverage (25
    MDAs) means that 20 internal audit teams are not provided the oversight needed to improve their independence
    and resolve reported issues. By mid-2019 15 audit committees had been reconstituted and a further 10 were added
    later in the project. An audit committee assessment performed by a consultant of 13 of the 15 earlier established
    MDA Audit Committees conducted in late 2021 found that over the 2018-2019 period in only 4 of the Audit
    Committees was there evidence that the prescribed quarterly meetings were held, and in two of these four they
    were unable to sustain the meetings after 2019.

47. The support provided to the Internal Audit Directorate on conducting risk-based audits has contributed to
    improvements to the effectiveness of internal audit as evidenced by PEFA assessment. The PEFA’s “effectiveness
    of internal audit” indicator, which initially was a PDO level indicator dropped following the MTR, notably improved
    between the PEFA assessment conducted in 2014 and that performed in 2018 and held constant between 2018 and
    2021 with some improvement noted for more risk-based audits.

48. The project helped support the capacity of the Audit Service Sierra Leone (ASSL) to perform performance audits
    of MDAs, but efforts to implement an Audit Management Information System (AMIS) to support effective and
    efficient external audits was hampered by an unsuccessful pilot. Support provided on performance audits has
    boosted the capacity of ASSL to assess value for money in Government expenditure. ASSL has not been able to
    identify an AMIS suitable for its needs and this work will continue under the new Accountable Governance for Basic
    Service Delivery Project.

49. The bottlenecks in resolving internal control and audit issues at MDAs raised by the Audit Service Sierra Leone is
    evidenced by the recent PEFA assessment. At the inception of the project there was a PDO indicator covering
    “Scope, nature, & follow-up of external audit” which was linked to a PEFA performance indicator with a baseline of
    C+ and a target of B and was dropped after the restructuring in 2017. According to the 2018 PEFA report, on a
    comparative basis with 2014 the indicator had not changed and remained C+, whereas the draft 2021 PEFA report



                                                                                                            Page 24 of 65
        The World Bank
       Public Financial Management Improvement and Consolidation Project (P133424)



    reflected the rating as having deteriorated to D+. The draft 2021 PEFA report identifies that “formal responses are
    not comprehensive as they fail to provide an action plan or road map for implementation of remedial actions”.

50. The project was effective in increasing citizen engagement in the PFM annual budget cycle. Citizen engagement to
    examine the executive budget and subsequently monitor its execution, including engaging on the resolution of audit
    recommendations was introduced as a project activity following the restructuring in 2017. The project continued to
    support a Non-State Actors (NSA) Secretariat established under the predecessor project (IPFMR)) at the Ministry of
    Finance which led the implementation of activities including 3 rounds of grants to 40 CSOs totaling 3.4b Le ($300k)
    and aimed to enhance citizen participation in key areas such as education and health. Significant work was also
    undertaken to try and address the issue of MDAs not responding to the audit recommendations of the Auditor
    General or internal audit and a study was performed based on the 2015/2016 audit recommendation as well as a
    pilot scorecard for 10 MDAs on audit follow-up and a 2018 citizen’s budget was developed. The PDO level indicator
    “non-state actor representatives and citizens participating in annual budget discussions” had a baseline of 50 and a
    target of 250 and by the end of the project this had been surpassed and recorded as 288.

51. Improved access to information to support enhanced citizen engagement was supported by the project. After
    the passing of the Rights to Access Information Act in 2013 a Rights to Access Information Commission (RAIC) was
    established and was supported by the project to develop regulations. Furthermore, training was provided to Access
    to Information Officers at 60 MDAs, and TA on a proactive disclosure scheme for 30 MDAs and 10 non-state actors.
    High priority datasets aligned with the SDGs at 5 selected ministries were published in the Open Data Portal.

52. Achieving increased citizen participation has been a significant achievement of the project, however, the NSA
    Secretariat wound-down activities before the end of the project and was subsequently unfunded although it
    remains unclear whether this has had any impact of ongoing engagement. While it remains to be seen if citizens
    will be less engaged in the PFM budget cycle considering the disbanded NSA Secretariat, it is notable that some of
    the good work started cannot be finished by the Secretariat and other avenues will need to be pursued to address
    the issues. For example, the NSA Secretariat was closely engaged on the lack of audit recommendations follow-up
    and produced a good set of related report recommendations. However, these recommendations have not been
    acted upon yet, although they have provided good input to Government’s work in this area, and the number of
    unresolved issues reported by the Auditor General remains high. Continued efforts will need to be made to keep the
    key non-sate actors actively involved to help support improvement in this area.

53. Effective Parliamentary scrutiny of the use of public finances has been hampered by perennial capacity
    constraints of the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) and while some improvements have been noted there is a
    significant deterioration in the resolution of reported audit issues. The project supported the PAC through the
    establishment of a PAC Secretariat which had three staff16 and vehicles17 and equipment to support public hearings.
    However, the extent of the PAC’s hearings and the timely publication of their work remains a challenge. It is also not
    clear how many of the recommendations that are published by the PAC are adequately resolved.

54. The PDO level indicator relating to the timely submission of the PAC’s work to Parliament was estimated to be
    achieved by the close of the project, but this was not the case. The timing of the work of the PAC was captured by

    the revised PDO indicator, the last ISR reported at mid-2021 that the time taken for the PAC to report to the
    legislature after receipt of the Auditor General’s report was estimated to have reached the target of 8 months. The
    estimation was required since the Auditor General would have delivered their 2019 report around mid-December of
    2020 meaning that the PAC would have until around mid-August 2021 to submit their report which was after the
    date of the last ISR. However, as at the date of this ICR the PAC has still not published its report on the 2019 (or the
    2020 report submitted by the Auditor General in December 2021 to Parliament) and, therefore, the target was not
    actually met. It is noted that apparently the report on the 2019 Auditor General’s report has apparently been
    completed but it is yet to be published due to a lack of funds to print the report which is a prerequisite for tabling
    the report in Parliament.

55. The impact of the project around legislative scrutiny of public expenditure was constrained by the lack of support
    by the Executive. Since the closure of the project, the PAC Secretariat has been disbanded due to a lack of funding.
    Over the course of the project several recommendations were provided on how to make the PAC more effective,
    however, these actions have not been able to be implemented due to a lack of political will which is reflected in the
    resources provided to the functioning of PAC.

56. The challenges faced by the PAC have been reflected in the PEFA assessments. The original set of PDO Indicators
    included the PEFA indicator “Extent of legislative scrutiny of audit reports (PI-28)” which had a baseline of C+ and
    was dropped following the MTR and replaced with the PDO indicator discussed in the preceding paragraph. The
    PEFA 2018 report assessed that the indicator had deteriorated compared to 2014 on a comparative basis going from
    C+ to D+ with the reasons cited for the decline including that it takes too long for the PAC to report back to
    Parliament and that the PAC is not conducting extensive hearings as part of their consideration of the Auditor
    General’s report. This indicator remained unchanged in the draft 2021 PEFA report.

57. While modest results were evidenced across internal audit, external audit, PAC, and citizen engagement, it is
    notable that perennial issues remain in resolving internal and external audit recommendations, which suggests
    that bottlenecks may be in the areas of coordination between accountability institutions and willingness of MDAs
    to address issues. Unresolved audit issues are important to resolve as they represent, in many cases, opportunities
    to improve the control environment at MDAs ultimately leading to more efficient and effective use of public
    finances and improved service delivery. However, a significant change is required in this area, and it will take time
    before results are seen. As a result of the awareness raised by the project of the issue, a policy trigger has been
    included in a subsequent DOP operation in Sierra Leone to develop Standard Operating Procedures for coordinating
    accountability institutions to work on having recommendations addressed.

58. The piloting of the Government Electronic Time and Attendance Management System (GETAMIS) will support the
    Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) manage employees working time and payroll management. The
    GETAMIS system was piloted at 20 MDAs and was supported by the project through the acquisition of required
    equipment and connectivity and comprehensive training for Permanent Secretaries, Human Resource Officers, ICT
    personnel and super administrative users. It is noted that in the 2021 draft PEFA assessment report that the
    “Payroll Controls” indicator improved significantly from D+ to B+ and although there were numerous areas of
    payroll reform, the automation of time and attendance is beneficial.

    Justification of Overall Efficacy Rating



                                                                                                                Page 26 of 65
        The World Bank
       Public Financial Management Improvement and Consolidation Project (P133424)



59. The overall efficacy rating is assessed as “Substantial”. The project’s achievement of the first overall outcome of
    improving the PFM system, which received over 80% of the project’s resources, was assessed as substantial.
    Expenditure management has been improved through the expansion of the IFMIS system across most of the MDAs,
    and revenue administration has been improved through the roll out of the ASYCUDA World system and the
    installation of a new ITAS system. More competition in procurement is a significant achievement, and although not
    implemented, preparation for an e-Procurement system has been advanced by the project and will be further
    supported under the new Accountable Governance for Basic Service Delivery project. The achievements in the
    second overall outcome of improving accountability in the use of Government finances were more modest and
    there are some lessons to be taken from this. The project was effective in increasing citizen engagement in public
    finances and building capacities of internal and external auditors, audit committees and the PAC, however, these
    efforts did not translate to improving the control environment at MDAs.



  C. EFFICIENCY

    Assessment of Efficiency and Rating

60. While the economic gains already captured and expected to flow from investment under the project are
    satisfactory, the implementation of the project encountered significant delays and the overall efficiency rating
    has been assessed as “Modest” accordingly. The benefits attributable to the results of the project are difficult to
    translate into economic inflows, however, these are estimated as supporting an efficient use of finances. On the
    other hand, delays in the implementation of the project were significant and as such project management costs
    were higher.

61. Despite the challenges in undertaking economic analysis of PFM reform projects, net benefits, and the cost of
    these are considered adequate. PFM reforms can lead to enhanced domestic revenue mobilization and
    effectiveness and efficiency in public expenditures and marginal economic gains early in reforming undeveloped
    PFM systems can be high and relatively less expensive than later marginal gains. However, complexities and
    significant variables including those associated with macro-economic factors make it difficult to isolate economic
    inflows associated with the investment. The PAD assessed economic viability through an NPV analysis of a part of
    the project including teacher emoluments, interest charges on ‘Ways and Means’ advances, and increases in
    revenues which showed a NPV of $11.6m. At the MTR, with the increased emphasis on revenue management
    systems (i.e., ITAS and ASYCUDA World) and associated revenue gains and transaction cost savings and gains from
    e-Procurement, the economic benefits were assessed as justifying the costs. The gains in customs revenues, the
    potential flows from improved indirect and direct taxation administration and the benefits from institutionalizing
    price norms in procurement, which Government has assessed as saving USD$7.7m since the beginning of 2019, will
    over time exceed the costs of the project without taking account of the ‘softer’ gains from improving budget,
    financial and oversight processes.

62. The costs related to financial and revenue management systems accounted for almost two-thirds of the project
    and were considered broadly comparable with similar systems enhancements on other projects. Systems roll-

                                                                                                               Page 27 of 65
        The World Bank
       Public Financial Management Improvement and Consolidation Project (P133424)



    outs amounted to approximately $17.3m USD or 60% of the project costs overall. The costs of IFMIS roll-out and
    ITAS implementation can be difficult to compare with other projects given the requirements may be different,
    however some comparative analysis was performed with the Liberia-Public Financial Management Reforms for
    Institutional Strengthening Project (P165000) which is currently under implementation. This project had similar
    objectives and approaches in respect to tax administration and IFMIS roll-out. The budgeted costs for “IFMIS
    Improvement and Rollout Support” amounts to $2.6m and $7.0m for “Capacity Development & Strengthening the
    Technological Basis for Tax Administration”. The IFMIS rollout cost under PFMICP was $6.9m and for the Liberian
    project the budget is $2.6m (or $3.7m in equivalent terms since it involves less MDAs). The difference appears to be
    explained by the starting point for the Liberian project being more amenable to the planned expansion, and it is also
    possible that some of the delays incurred under PFMICP increased the cost. For ITAS, the PFMICP project combined
    the costs of ASYCUDA World and ITAS implementations, however, the contract cost for installation of ITAS was
    $5.7m which seems reasonably comparable to the Liberian project. Delays in ITAS installation were subject to no-
    cost extensions which helped contain costs.

63. A variety of significant implementation delays, some of which were within the control of the project’s
    management and some of which were not, have substantially impaired project efficiency. The implementation
    efficiency of the project was hampered by implementation delays mainly relating to procurement processes. The
    project was subject to three no-cost extensions to ensure the new systems were properly embedded, the first
    extended the project to 31 March 2020, the second was a one-year extension to 31 March 2021 and finally the
    project was extended for a further six months to 30 September 2021. The first no-cost extension was provided to
    afford time to implement new activities resulting from the first restructuring at MTR, the second and third were
    cited as being mostly related to COVID-19.

64. The roll-out of IFMIS to the planned 54 MDAs was not achieved until mid-2019 representing around two years of
    delay. The IFMIS rollout was hampered by delays in contracting a project manager and embedding the
    recommended governance structure and practices. Various technical challenges were encountered including delays
    in setting up the supporting IT infrastructure and migrating MDAs onto the Government network.

65. The ITAS system implementation which was expected to be completed before 30 March 2020 was delayed by a
    year and a half. The initial procurement of the ITAS system was cancelled due to lapses in the procurement process
    and the contract was not signed until October 2018 following a new procurement. Delivery of the ITAS system so
    late in the project has increased the risk that expected gains may not materialize since post-project optimization
    (e.g., troubleshooting and process training) is not adequately managed and financed.

66. The e-Procurement system implementation did not materialize under the project as initially planned and will be
    undertaken under the new Accountable Governance for Basic Service Delivery Project. Although a consultant was
    hired under the project to develop a roadmap for e-Procurement implementation in January 2018, delays in
    establishing a functional e-GP Directorate and defining the requirements of the e-Procurement system meant that
    the original objective could not be delivered within the project’s timeframe, despite the project being effective for
    seven years, and the focus subsequently changed to preparing for procurement of the system under the subsequent
    project.

67. Project management and coordination initially budgeted to cost $2.4m ended up costing $4.0m and increase of
    $1.6m with such costs representing almost 14% of the overall project costs which is considered high. While the
    budget for the project management and coordination was not changed by the additional financing, the eventual
    costs exceeded the budget. It is noted that the related component formed part of a broader withdrawal category
    which meant that such overruns did not require a restructure.

    D. JUSTIFICATION OF OVERALL OUTCOME RATING

68. The relevance of the PDO is “High”, the efficacy in achieving the PDO is “Substantial”, and the efficiency is
    “Modest”. Based on these ratings, the overall outcome is assessed as “Moderately Satisfactory”.

  E. OTHER OUTCOMES AND IMPACTS (IF ANY)

     Gender
69. The project did not have significant outcomes or impacts on gender; however, some support was provided to
    train 60 women and youth in the Western Area to monitor service delivery in their localities and enhance the
    professional development of female procurement practitioners. The original PDO Indicator set included a target of
    600 Female beneficiaries, however, this was dropped at the project’s MTR. The number of female public
    procurement professionals who were trained was an intermediate indicator which was not met. Under the NSA
    grants, four18 women’s advocacy groups were supported, and of note was the support provided to the Foundation
    for the Financial and Economic Inclusion who trained 60 women and youth on PFM, service delivery, advocacy and
    monitoring to empower them to engage on monitoring service delivery at their localities.

70. The PEFA assessment included undertaking a supplementary assessment to cover “Gender Responsive Public
    Financial Management” (GRPFM) for the 2020 fiscal year, the draft PEFA reports concludes that Government’s
    performance in this area is poor. In the context of the PEFA assessment being a monitoring tool for the progress
    made under the PFMICP project, it is important to note the poor performance of Gender responsive Public Financial
    Management in Sierra Leone. The GRPFM assessment covers 9 performance indicators19 and the assessment was
    “D” in every single indicator and sub-indicator and underscore the opportunities to incorporate more gender
    responsive outcomes in future PFM projects.



    Institutional Strengthening
71. The project has helped to strengthen institutions involved in the planning, execution, and oversight of PFM. The
    legal and institutional framework for PFM went through a passage of modernization with the passing of the PFM Act
    2016, the PFM Regulations 2018, and a few other related laws. While the legal framework for PFM in Sierra Leone

    is reasonably sound, capacity to implement requirements needs improvement. In this context the project provided
    substantial support to various units within the MoF, which is charged with the management of the annual budget
    cycle, and the accountability institutions charged with monitoring the use of public funds, including the ASSL, the
    PAC and the internal audit function including the audit committees. Cross-cutting in the project’s approach to
    institutional strengthening was putting new technologies in place to support better service delivery and efficient
    resource allocation. New units were also established with the support of the project including the PAC Secretariat,
    and the e-GP Directorate along with the reconstitution of 15 audit committees at the MDAs.
72. While the approach taken to institutional strengthening was sound, more work could have been done to
    mainstream annual budget finance for oversight, review the effectiveness of new PFM laws, and place more focus
    on PFM at the MDAs. As noted through the assessment of the project’s efficacy, some important areas of the PFM
    are constrained by a lack of resources (e.g., funding for the operation of reconstituted audit committees, funding
    the PAC Secretariat, funding the support to coordinate citizen engagement by non-state actor, etc.). More focus on
    advocating and increasing resource allocation to these areas may have benefited the longevity of the outcomes the
    project achieved. Since the passing of the PFM laws several ‘reactionary’ amendments have been made to address
    issues arising, for example, to provide for a basis to sanction public officials not implementing audit
    recommendations. While these changes are welcomed, it might have been effective for the project to support a
    post-implementation review of the PFM Act to identify amendments that may support stronger PFM. Lastly, the
    project did not place much emphasis on the MDAs themselves and their capacity to improve their control
    environments, which become evident as the number of unresolved audit recommendations grew.



    Mobilizing Private Sector Financing
73. There was no direct mobilization of private sector financing in the implementation of the project’s activity.
    However, improving the quality and transparency of Government’s audited financial information can help the
    private sector gain confidence and obtain decision useful information to support more private sector participation
    in the national development agenda.



   Poverty Reduction and Shared Prosperity
74. PFM plays an important role in ensuring public funds can be applied efficiently and effectively on development
    programs that can help lift people out of poverty, and in delivering basic services. Also, poor fiscal discipline can
    lead to rising debt costs the servicing of which can crowd-out development spending. While the project has made
    useful contributions, overall PFM systems are relatively basic and further improvements are needed to support
    national development programs more fully.



    Other Unintended Outcomes and Impacts
75. None noted.

III. KEY FACTORS THAT AFFECTED IMPLEMENTATION AND OUTCOME

 A. KEY FACTORS DURING PREPARATION

       76. The project preparation benefited from an ongoing PEFA Assessment which helped to identify key
           priorities for PFM reform support. The PEFA Assessment of 2014 was in-process during preparation,
           and this supported defining priorities and shaping the results framework for both the Government
           and the Bank.

       77. The predecessor project “Integrated Public Financial Management Reform Project” closed in 2014
           and the results of this project also helped with the preparation of the project. The predecessor
           project informed both Government and the Bank in preparing the successor project.


 B. KEY FACTORS DURING IMPLEMENTATION

       78. Some of the key factors that contributed to the delay in implementation of project activities
           included:

              a. Complexity of Project Design: The project was designed with five components and twenty-
                 five sub-components. Inclusion of some of the sub-components was based not on objectives
                 but the competing interests of the stakeholders. This structural complexity complicated
                 coordination and implementation of activities on the client side and tracking of progress
                 through monthly reports on the donor side.

              b. Project Effectiveness delay: Effectiveness date was pushed forward by almost nine months
                 to 19 September 2014, owing to the government’s inability to meet some of the core
                 requirements, such as the hiring of an International Procurement Specialist.

              c. Macroeconomic Challenges: Consumer price inflation continues to increase overall driven by
                 commodity price volatilities and the local currency, the Sierra Leonean Leone, continues to
                 depreciate particularly against the US Dollar. The Ebola epidemic and COVID-19 pandemic
                 have increased Government’s expenditure and the cost of servicing debt is crowding-out
                 expenditures in important service delivery areas. The economy continues to be dependent
                 on extractives and in need of economic diversification.

              d. Ebola outbreak (2014-2016): The outbreak took place immediately after project
                 effectiveness (September 2014) and caused about nine months implementation delay. Cost
                 over-run: The Integrated Taxation Administration System (ITAS) was expected to be
                 undertaken earlier on within the project; however, the onset of Ebola took away the
                 preparatory time and resulted in increased cost of the system which could not be fully met
                 through project cost savings (as envisaged). This resulted in the cancellation of the initial



                                                                                                           Page 31 of 65
      The World Bank
     Public Financial Management Improvement and Consolidation Project (P133424)



                   ITAS procurement, its re-launch, and further delays in processing approval of the ITAS
                   Project Plan.

              e. Change of government (in May 2018): The new administration caused delays in accepting
                 some of the project activities (for example the E-procurement implementation).

              f.   Withdrawal of committed funds: The MDTF was anticipated to provide US$13m in funding
                   but with the withdrawal of the EU and FCDO the funding achieved was only US$4,6m. The
                   unexpected withdrawal of the two development partners in providing committed funds
                   created financing gap which was resolved through AF following the project’s restructuring at
                   MTR.

              g. Change of Administration in early 2018: With a change in Administration in April 2018 the
                 project experienced some delays as the remaining project activities were subject to scrutiny
                 and it took time for acceptance. The e-Procurement initiative was especially delayed
                 because of this change.

              h. COVID 19 outbreak: Activities were put on hold during nationwide lockdown due to COVID-
                 19; as a result, the project had to be further extended by six months to complete all project
                 activities. Further COVID 19 related restrictions constrained economic activities and
                 impacted revenue collections.

              i.   Technical capacity to manage significant PFM reform was constrained despite the project’s
                   efforts to support parallel endeavors to reduce constraints through training.

              j.   On several occasions throughout implementation, ineffective procurement processes and
                   contract management lead to procurement and implementation delays.

              k. Delays in hiring key positions for project implementation and high turnover/attrition rates of
                 key technical assistance consultants.

      79. IT systems implementation was hampered by inadequate governance structures to monitor
          progress and make decisions to address issues in a timely manner. All planned systems
          implementation were either delayed (IFMIS, ITAS, EDMA and EFT) or were unable to be completed
          during the implementation period (e-Procurement). Issues were encountered in the governance and
          contract management of these projects, including significant problems with procurement processes.


IV. BANK PERFORMANCE, COMPLIANCE ISSUES, AND RISK TO DEVELOPMENT OUTCOME

 A. QUALITY OF MONITORING AND EVALUATION (M&E)

   M&E Design




                                                                                                            Page 32 of 65
       The World Bank
      Public Financial Management Improvement and Consolidation Project (P133424)



80. The progress towards the PDO was monitored through the PDO level and intermediate level results indicators, as
    presented in Annex 1 of the Project Appraisal Document, and revised during project restructurings in 2017, 2019
    and 2021 and was conducted on a continuous basis under the leadership coordination of the PMU (MoF). The full-
    time M&E Specialist under the PMU oversaw coordination, soliciting and deriving the primary data and information
    to report on project progress against the agreed upon baselines, annual targets, frequency of data collections, data
    source and methodology, by responsible institutions for data collection for all indicators.

81. The PDO and intermediate indicators were changed through the project’s implementation and broadly reflected
    progress toward the achievement of the project’s development objective, although not all indicators were a good
    measure of results. The quality of the results framework was reasonable overall but did not always reflect
    performance toward achieving the development objective. For example, the PDO Indicator on the timeliness of
    submission of the PAC report to Parliament did not reflect the work done by the PAC in resolving audit
    recommendations made by the Auditor General which remains a significant issue. Also, the indicator on the ratio
    between “actual primary expenditure and original budgeted primary expenditure” did not account for budget
    composition variances which remain an issue in Sierra Leone.


   M&E Implementation


82. Reviews of implementation progress were undertaken regularly on a monthly, quarterly, and annual basis and
    the results were used to discuss issues and bottlenecks that emerged from time-to-time impeding achievement of
    targeted outcomes. The issues raised were also discussed by project management and the Bank implementation
    support missions and resulting recommendations became action points for implementation follow-up and
    subsequent implementation support. The government's PFM Reforms Steering Committee regularly received and
    reviewed the strategic information on implementation progress from the PMU and the PFMRU, in respect of the
    entire PFM reforms program in the country and provided strategic guidance to enable the project to achieve its
    development objectives. Trained staff was engaged to collect and analyze field data while the PMU oversaw the
    overall progress and provided timely information to assist in the effective decision-making process. The M&E unit
    regularly sent to the World Bank detailed progress reports with up-to-date highlights of the reporting period. The
    Bank teams in turn provided continuous guidance to enable components move forward. Reporting templates were
    developed and used for reporting that captured necessary information from each component.

83. Progress reporting during the first half of the project required improvement to be more comprehensive by being
    more focused on results reporting rather than activities. Training was provided to focal persons at the
    implementing partners to assist them with how to manage results. More focus on allocating project resources to
    support M&E helped to operationalize the results framework, but overall, the quality of reporting, while adequate,
    could have been more results driven. The monitoring of the ITAS implementation was hampered by a lack of clear
    information on progress.


    M&E Utilization
84. The M&E framework provided good input for the reallocation of priorities and funding effected through project
    restructurings. The project was restructured in 2017, 2019 and 2021 based on the reviews of the indicators

   monitored and revisions were made. Some indicators were modified while others were deleted and the evaluation
   of progress at the MTR supported changes to the project to focus on a more consolidated set of priorities.



    Justification of Overall Rating of Quality of M&E
85. The overall rating of M&E quality is considered “Modest.” The M&E framework and system as designed and
    implemented was broadly adequate to assess the achievements of the project objectives comprehensively and

B. ENVIRONMENTAL, SOCIAL, AND FIDUCIARY COMPLIANCE

       86. Environmental Safeguards: As a Category 'C' Technical Assistance project, which by its nature does
           not have any environmental safeguard issues. None of Bank's environmental safeguard policies
           were triggered, either at project appraisal stage, or during Additional Financing. The environmental
           category remained at ‘C’.

       87. Social Safeguards: Given the focus on improving budget planning and financial control and
           accountability, the project did not trigger any social safeguards policies. In fact, the social impacts of
           the project are expected to be positive, as it will increase the timely disclosure of overall
           government revenue collections, improve the link between procurement and budget, and improve
           procurement efficiency, all leading to greater accountability. The project was processed under
           safeguards and not ESF.

       88. Fiduciary: The project’s financial management risk was rated as Substantial at appraisal. The risk
           was mitigated by the establishment of a Project Coordinating Unit manned by professionally
           recruited and qualified personnel to handle the day-to-day project management for the
           Government of Sierra Leone and close supervision by the Bank’s FM Specialist as well as provision of
           appropriate support to the Project Finance Team Members. Most of the time the audits were
           undertaken, and interim financial reports prepared in a relatively timely manner. Compliance with
           provisions of the operational manual as well as other positive FM actions had the project maintain
           an ISR rating of satisfactory for most part of the project. The PMU Finance Unit, headed by a
           Qualified Accountant maintained an adequate financial management system in compliance with the
           World Bank’s Financing Agreement and/or other relevant legal documents to support the
           deployment of project resources in an economic, efficient, and effective manner to achieve the
           desired objective. Fixed assets register was maintained and updated subjected to regular
           verification of assets’ location and condition. The project was able to ensure adequate liquidity by
           preparing quarterly forecasts and submit Withdrawal Applications for replenishment of funds to the
           Designated Account. The Special Accounts were reconciled periodically, and timely bank
           reconciliation statements were produced. Reviews including midterm and in-depth reviews by the
           World Bank were conducted with relatively few reported issues.

 C. BANK PERFORMANCE

    Quality at Entry
89. The project’s design reflected Government’s PFM reform priorities and benefited from input from PEFA
    assessments and lessons from the predecessor project. The project’s components captured many of the key
    reform areas and were broad in scope covering most elements of the annual budget cycle.
90. The extensive number of sub-components meant that there were many stakeholders required to be coordinated
    and activities to manage, it ended up being overly ambitious and the project was restructured mid-term. The
    project was designed with five components and twenty-five sub-components, involve PFM at the central and local
    government level and involved many implementing partners. The government’s capacity to implement the project
    as designed was not sufficient and this was reflected early with implementation delays with some areas not
    progressing at all.

91. The project did not appear to address an important set of binding constraints in the functioning of the PFM
    system through project design, which may have contributed to more impact. There are a number of known areas
    that constrain the PFM environment in Sierra Leone that could have been factored into the project’s design, some
    of which were recognized in the PPAR of the predecessor project20. These include:

        a. The existence of extra-budgetary funds that are not subject to parliamentary scrutiny (including for example
           the use of donor funds).

        b. Supplementary budgets that are not submitted to Parliament and include variations on approved budgets.

        c. Centralized payment approval processes that lead to inefficiencies.

        d. Connectivity issues that limit the likelihood that citizens can access information published on websites and
           open data portals.

        e. SAI dependence on the MoF for adequate funding.

        f.   Weak risk management and control environment at MDAs and local councils

        g. Low buy-in for an enhanced role for audit committees and enhanced PAC scrutiny


92. The results framework could have been more focused in areas of budget reliability, management of assets and
    liabilities, and the effectiveness of accountability institutions. The results framework tended to capture
    performance indicators and targets that did not reflect the change needed to improve the key issues in PFM in
    Sierra Leone. For example, although budget inputs were well covered there was little focus on the reliability of the
    budget. Also, the key area of public investment management was not reflected in the results framework at any
    stage despite the perennial poor performance in this area and the importance of it in supporting the country’s
    national development plan. Lastly, the establishment of audit committees at MDAs and their regular meetings did

    not reflect progress towards the implementation of audit recommendations by ASSL. Given the project’s initial
    design, which seemed overly ambitious, it appears that more concentration on key areas was lacking.


93. Delays in meeting effectiveness conditions got the project off to a poor start and the Bank may have
    overestimated the Government’s capacity to meet the Effectiveness Conditions and not consider alternatives. The
    project was approved by the Board on 27 November 2013 but did not become effective until 30 September 2014
    following delays in meeting the Effectiveness Conditions, it is possible that the delay may have been avoided if more
    assessment was performed on the capacity of government to achieve the deadlines and if alternatives ways were
    considered for handling the various conditions.


    Quality of Supervision
94. Regular implementation support missions were undertaken throughout the project, and the issues identified
    were promptly addressed and adequately reported in aide memoires, ISRs and management letters. The
    frequency and quality of supervision appeared to be satisfactory. The average annual supervision cost was US$
    230,510 and an average of 29.4 staff weeks per year. The Aide Memoires and Management Letters captured
    implementation issues and set out comprehensively the issues and decisions taken, and the action plan agreed
    (which was extensively followed-up in the following missions). The Bank responded quickly and effectively to issues
    that arose. For the 2020 and 2021 project supervision was impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic and resulting
    restrictions of travel, however, this did not appear to have much negative impact and the virtual missions
    undertaken were effective in monitoring progress and addressing implementation issues. The task team needs to be
    credited for undertaking missions during the Ebola crisis and undertaking effective remote supervision during the
    COVID-19 travel ban and successfully closing the project virtually.


    Justification of Overall Rating of Bank Performance
95. The overall rating for the Bank’s performance is “Moderately Satisfactory”. Overall, the Bank team persistently
    addressed challenges that arose through preparation and implementation and provided useful technical support
    and proactive support in a timely manner. The quality on entry was hampered by designing a project that appeared
    overly ambitious in the context of the Government’s capacity to implement.


 D. RISK TO DEVELOPMENT OUTCOME

96. The ITAS system was not operationally accepted until the end of the project and the results may be
    impacted if full utilization of the system is not properly managed in the post-implementation period. The
    ITAS system was installed and operationally accepted at the close of the project. Results to the date of the
    ICR are highlighted in Under pilot registration, 507 taxpayers had been registered. Sustainability of revenue
    systems (ITAS and ASYCUDA World) is crucial for success of the higher-level goal of generating more
    revenues. For this reason, it is important that further consideration is given by Government for the safety
    and continued use of these systems and the data generated from them. Specifically, for these revenue
    systems to be sustainable, there is need for follow-up investment in the following:

      a. Creation of a Disaster Recovery Site as an alternative back up site for safety of the new revenue

      b. Creation of a Data Warehouse System to serve as a central repository of data from the revenue

         and cleaned data can be extracted for compliance monitoring, risk analysis, audit selectivity, and

         generated from the basic service and compliance revenue systems. Currently, NRA hired a Data

         software capacity can only accommodate the current taxpayer numbers and staff utilization. As

     in power such change may impact on the successful continuation of the work under the PFMICP
     project.

 98. A lack of adequate funding provided to accountability institutions and a lack of effective
     incentives for compliance of PFM requirements by MDAs and Local Councils could undermine the
     progress made on addressing audit recommendations. Funding provided by the project to support
     the NSA Secretariat, the PAC and the audit committees has not to date been mainstreamed into the
     annual budget (partially for audit committee). Without adequate funding these important
     institutions may not be able to undertake their role effectively and the extent of unresolved audit
     recommendations will not likely improve. Furthermore, incentives to ensure that Vote Controllers

          comply with risk management, internal control provisions and financial reporting requirements of
          the PFM Act, and timely resolution of audit recommendations of ASSL and internal auditors, need to

          sanctioning for not resolving recommendations, however, it is unclear how this provision will be
          operated (or whether it can be in current form).

V. LESSONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS

          PFM Related

      99. PFM reform is broad scope, with many interdependent technical elements, many key stakeholders
          across levels of government and sectors. Coordinating PFM reform is a significant challenge given
          its technical complexities and vested interests. It is essential that there is a PFM reform strategy
          that is cohesive and with which all stakeholders are fully engaged. Having committed focal points
          on the Government side is essential as is well structured governance systems that oversee reforms
          with instituted effective feedback loops such that they can address issues quickly and adjust their
          strategy and/or its sequencing where required based on evolving circumstances. Having citizens
          engaged in PFM reform is also critical so that they can hold Government accountable for achieving
          the plan and offer technical advice. The more ‘strategic’ aspects PFM Reform including reform
          governance, monitoring progress dynamically in real-time, and engaging citizen groups as part of the
          reform process, as distinct from their involvement in the PFM system, requires capacity
          enhancements and can be intentionally factored into PFM project design.

      100. PFM reforms are a long-term undertaking with incremental gains even with more modern IT
         technologies to support improvements. Despite the modernization of technologies that can
         support PFM systems, implementing such technologies is very challenging especially in fragile
         contexts. Ensuring that systems can be seamlessly introduced into IT environments, having a clear
         description of ‘business processes’ and, therefore, requirements, training users to be able to benefit
         from the full functionality of the system, maintaining the IT environment and user support post-
         implementation to continue utilization and ensuring stakeholders (e.g., external auditors), also build
         capacity to undertake their role effectively in the PFM system (e.g., computer audits). Therefore, in
         addition to the ‘normal’ complexity of PFM reforms, including IT system implementation and roll-out
         into a PFM project requires careful planning, effective governance structures, effective coordination
         between entities and a clear ‘champion’ within Government.

      101. Ambitious projects that involve a wide range of highly technical areas, as is the case with PFM,
         require a careful assessment of the Government’s capacity to implement the project and a
         responsive and ongoing plan for comprehensive strategic, technical and project implementation
         TA support. The PFMICP project was very ambitious with many complex elements. Attempting to
         address significant capacity constraints during implementation can increase the risk of delays and
         non-achievement of the development objective. Some balance is needed between sequencing
         activities to align with capacity developing and building capacity in parallel with project
         implementation. Further balance is required to be more strategic in selecting activities that have


                                                                                                          Page 38 of 65
 The World Bank
Public Financial Management Improvement and Consolidation Project (P133424)



     highest impact and a realistic view of Government’s capacity to achieve such activities in a
     reasonable timeframe. It is also important when using PEFA indicators to have supplementary
     indicators that measure targeted performance areas that are linked to the assessed constraints to
     achieving the key results, this provides a clear focus on linking improving capacity to achieving
     results.

 102. Constraints in addressing the resolution of audit issues at MDAs should have been addressed
    more extensively with more of a focus early on and should not have been left unaddressed at the
    end of the project. With the passing of the PFM Act 2016 and associated 2018 regulations the PFM
    legal framework is reasonable, however, the capacity to implement the requirements is varied. In
    addition to building capacities to comply it is important to ensure that appropriate incentives are
    reflected to ensure MDAs comply, such as by including sanctions for no-compliance. Additionally,
    while the legal framework is adequate the coordination required by accountability institutions to
    follow-up on reported issues is lacking and the DPF operation in Sierra Leone is currently aiming to
    support this.

 103. The PEFA supplemental assessment on gender response public financial management
    demonstrated that there is a significant opportunity for PFM reform in Sierra Leone to be more
    gender responsive. The PEFA supplemental assessment in Sierra Leone has highlighted
    opportunities for Government to make the PFM process more gender responsive, this should be
    considered for further PFM reform initiatives and Bank PFM projects.

     General

 104. Continued focus is required to address procurement and contract management challenges on
    development projects in Sierra Leone. Procurement delays and lapses and contract management
    issues caused significant delays in project implementation, it is important that the Bank maintains its
    dialogue with the Government on how to address these constraints and issues.

 105. Attention to effective co-financier and donor coordination is important to avoid funding gaps
    and fragmented support to Government to implement its reforms. It is essential that donor
    collaboration and coordination is effective to gain synergies and maximize development support to
    Government. This includes having a collective understanding of reform priorities and shared
    expectations about what the project can achieve. The EU and FCDO withdrew from the PFMICP
    project early in project implementation, due to a change in their priorities, and this created a
    funding gap that was required to be filled and led to parallel donor activities in the PFM sphere.

 106. There should have been a plan to mainstream institutional recurrent costs, financed under the
    project, into the annual budget so that activities could be sustained after the project. The project
    financed the costs of the PAC Secretariat which was immediately decommissioned at the project’s
    close. Similarly, the project financed a Non-State Actors Secretariat which accomplished some good
    results such as the 2018 citizens budget and the management of the NSA Grants, once the funding
    stopped the NSA Secretariat was dissolved. The operation of the audit committees of the initial 15
    reconstituted was also financed under the project and it is essential that their funding is retained

         ensuring that risks are identified and mitigated, and that internal control and other audit issues are
         adequately resolved in a timely way. Inadequate allocation of funds to institutions such as these
         creates a funding imbalance between management of the PFM cycle and its oversight.

Countries around the world
adopted a wide range of
fiscal measures in 2020
to mitigate the health and
economic impacts of the
COVID-19 pandemic.

At the beginning of 2021, the outlook on
the evolution of the pandemic remains
uncertain. COVID-19 cases are at high
levels in many countries, but effective
vaccines have been approved and are
being rolled out. The occurrence of new
variants of the virus that spread more
easily and more quickly and that may
be associated with an increased risk of
death adds to the uncertainty as to how
quickly the pandemic can be brought
under control.

Acknowledgements
This note was prepared by Robert Utz, Eric Lacey, and Joseph Massad under the guidance
and supervision of Chiara Bronchi. It builds on an earlier note on “The fiscal impact and policy
response to COVID-19.” Work on the assessment of policy action was initiated by Marijn Ver-
hoeven and the fiscal policy action data base was updated by Olawunmi Ifetola Ola Busari.
Massimo Mastruzzi, Sebastian James, and Jaffar Al Rikabi provided helpful discussion and
feedback during the preparation of the note. The note also benefitted from feedback of staff
from the Fiscal Policy and Sustainable Growth Unit at a presentation of the draft note. Brittany
Walters and Mark Volk edited the report.




                                                A REVIEW OF FISCAL POLICY RESPONSES TO COVID-19   <<<       5
 7.
MTI INSIGHT
 >>>
A Review of Fiscal Policy
Responses to COVID-19
>>	INTRODUCTION
   Countries around the world adopted a wide range of fiscal measures in 2020 to mitigate the
health and economic impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic. At the beginning of 2021, the outlook
on the evolution of the pandemic remains uncertain. COVID-19 cases are at high levels in many
countries, but effective vaccines have been approved and are being rolled out. The occurrence
of new variants of the virus that spread more easily and more quickly and that may be associated
with an increased risk of death1 adds to the uncertainty as to how quickly the pandemic can be
brought under control.

   Policymakers face difficult choices in an environment where fiscal space may be narrowing
and additional spending requirements emerge, including for the purchase and distribution of
vaccines as well as for measures to support an economic recovery. Which of the policy measures
already in place need to be maintained and extended and which ones can be phased out? Which
new measures need to be added? Where are opportunities for greater efficiency and better
targeting? What needs to be done to recover funds from measures such as tax deferrals and
loans?

  This note and the associated COVID-19 fiscal policy measures dashboard are intended to
help inform answers to these questions by providing analysis of the countries’ portfolios of fiscal
policy measures adopted in 2020 and their key characteristics such as reversibility of policy
measures, possibility of cost recovery, or targetability. This note allows policymakers to better
understand which policies and types of policies follow international best practice and which
measures should be carefully monitored in the implementation or closure phases. In all, about
4000 policy actions adopted by 203 economies have been grouped into seven categories and
47 sub-categories. About half of these policy actions have been judged as to whether they met
the criteria across nine dimensions, including targetability, speed, abuse resistance, affordability,
predictability and cost control, scalability, reversibility, administrative complexity, and feasibility
considering social distancing and contagion risks.

  The dashboard offers a flexible tool to analyze country portfolios of fiscal policy measures by
categories, sub-categories, and characteristics of measures and it allows comparison with other
country or country groupings.

1.	   CDC. 2021. New Variants of the Virus that Causes COVID-19. Updated Feb. 2, 2021. https://www.cdc.gov/coronavi-
      rus/2019-ncov/transmission/variant.html. Accessed on Feb. 14, 2021.


                                                           A REVIEW OF FISCAL POLICY RESPONSES TO COVID-19         <<<   7
 >>>	 ORGANIZATION OF THE PAPER

   The paper consists of three parts. The first part provides an overview of the fiscal response to the COVID-19 pandemic from
its onset until September 2020. We look at the different types of interventions that countries have adopted to mitigate the health
impacts of the pandemic and to support households and businesses. The second part of the paper reviews the characteristics of
fiscal policy interventions, using the policy assessment framework from Fiscal Impact and Policy Response to COVID-19 (World
Bank 2020) as a guide. The third part of the paper concludes by drawing lessons for continued efforts to mitigate the health and
economic impacts of the pandemic and the implications for transitioning from the current set of crisis response measures to support
for economic recovery and fiscal stabilization. Six annexes describe the methodology used to assess fiscal policy interventions
and offer a deep look into topics that are referenced throughout the paper, including a post-scoring diagnostic of the scoring
methodology, a list of country-level scores on the nine policy dimensions of the policy assessment framework, ANOVA statistical
analysis of variance of scores across country groups, logistic regression analysis assessing the marginal impact of policies and
country characteristics on policy dimension scores, pairwise correlation tables of policy scores, and the full table of Assessment
Options for Fiscal Policy Measures from the fiscal policy paper.

>>	         OVERVIEW OF FISCAL POLICY INTERVENTIONS
>>>	 A FAST AND SIZABLE RESPONSE                                                        and revenue measures and 2.5 percent of GDP in support
                                                                                        through other instruments. Low income developing countries
  On March 11, 2020, the World Health Organization declared                             (LIDCs) had the smallest fiscal response at about 1.8 percent
COVID-19 a pandemic. Most countries followed the advice                                 of GDP which was almost entirely in the form of expenditure
of health experts and quickly introduced strict containment                             and revenue measures (1.6 percent of GDP).

of the virus. The Oxford Stringency Index captures the                                  > > >

of calendar year 2020. Between March 9, 2020 and April 1,
2020, the world became serious about controlling the spread
of the virus (Figure 1). Although these containment measures
were necessary from a health perspective, they contributed
to a sudden reduction of income for many businesses
and individuals, led to a sharp increase in unemployment
worldwide, and raised serious concerns not just about the
short-term liquidity of businesses, but their long-term solvency.
The need for measures to protect livelihoods and secure
business assets was clear.

   Most governments quickly introduced fiscal policy measures
to provide financial support to businesses and households, and

pandemic. The magnitude of the fiscal response and the type
of instruments differs significantly by country groupings (Figure
2). Advanced economies’ (AEs) crisis response amounted
to more than 9 percent of GDP in expenditure and revenue
measures and another 11 percent in support through equity
and loans, guarantees, and quasi-fiscal activities. Emerging
markets and middle-income economies’ (EMMIEs) response
is more muted, but they also deployed all instruments for the
fiscal response, with about 3.4 percent of GDP in expenditure

3.	   The data on policy responses to COVID-19 used in this report were taken from the Fiscal Policy and Sustainable Growth Unit’s (FPU) Fiscal Policy Matrix, last updated
      on September 1, 2020. The data cover 203 economies and comprise nearly 4,000 fiscal policy measures launched or announced in response to the COVID-19 pan-
      demic. The policies were gathered in two waves: from the onset of the pandemic through May 1, 2020 (amounting to 2,400 policies), and then from May 1, 2020 until
      September 1, 2020 (totaling 1,591 policies). The main sources referenced to compile our dataset were the IMF, OECD, IBDF, and the Doing Business policy tracker
      databases. Other data featured in this report are from the World Development Indicators, the ICTD Government Revenue Dataset, the World Health Organization, the
      University of Oxford, and the October 2020 IMF Fiscal Monitor.
4.    About 1,500 of the nearly 4,000 fiscal measures accounted for in our database were of the exact same type as measures previously implemented by the same country.
      Some of these measures were not exactly repeats; rather, they were benefits which had been previously offered to one demographic extended to new demographics.
      Many were revisions to early policies, extending benefits beyond the originally planned end date, or scaling up spending on health measures as need evolved further.
5.    IMF policy tracker and IMF Fiscal Monitor (October, 2020).


                                                                                                          A REVIEW OF FISCAL POLICY RESPONSES TO COVID-19            <<<      9

                                                                                                         A REVIEW OF FISCAL POLICY RESPONSES TO COVID-19         <<<   11
 >>>	 A CORE SET OF FISCAL POLICY RESPONSES WAS ADOPTED BY MOST COUNTRIES

  The objectives of fiscal policy actions adopted by countries fall into three broad groups: (a) to deal with the health pandemic,
(b) to support households and (c) to bolster businesses. Governments pursue these objectives by using the whole range of fiscal
instruments, including tax and expenditure measures, credits, and guarantees, according to which we group policy actions into
seven broad categories and 47 sub-categories.

7.	   Note the three objectives (each column), the seven categories of measure (color coding), and the 47 distinct sub-categories.


12    >>>   A REVIEW OF FISCAL POLICY RESPONSES TO COVID-19
   Figure 5 shows the number of categories addressed by each country’s fiscal policy response. Some high- and upper-middle
income countries stand out for their multi-dimensional responses, engaging multiple of the following categories: revenue measures
for businesses, revenue measures for the health system, revenue measures for households, expenditure measures for businesses,
expenditure measures for the health system, expenditure measures for households, and credit and equity measures. Europe,
North and South America, and Oceanic Southeast Asia demonstrate a tendency to approach the fiscal response from multiple
angles. Sub-Saharan Africa and Central America favored generally narrow approaches, with the notable exceptions of Niger and
South Africa. Readers will notice a pronounced but imperfect relationship between income and the multi-dimensionality of the fiscal
policy response.

> > >
F I G U R E 5 - Multi-dimensionality8 of the Fiscal Policy Reponse as of September 1, 2020




      Source: World Bank Fiscal Policy data base, January – September 2020.




8.	    The seven categories correspond to the seven identified in Table 2.

                                                                                A REVIEW OF FISCAL POLICY RESPONSES TO COVID-19   <<< 13
 > > >
T A B L E 2 - The Five Most Frequent Fiscal Inverventions by Country Groupings and Regions (% of Countries
Implementing Measure in Category)




Source: Original calculations for this publication.


  Of the 47 different types of fiscal policy measures identified in this study, most countries and country groups implemented 8-12
types of measures, and often a similar mix. The most common measures were typically found in all country groups and include
general health spending, loans and deferral of tax payments for businesses, and direct cash transfers and supplementary ad-hoc
programs for households (Table 2).

   Readers may also wish to consider Table 3, which shows detailed information on the share of countries implementing each type
of policy by country group. Some country groups gravitate strongly toward particular policies more than other country groups do.
For instance, low- and lower-middle income countries, FCVs, countries collecting tax revenue below 15 percent of GDP, and Sub-
Saharan Africa favored health expenditure measures far more than other measures. This was likely an effort to prioritize capacity
building in the health sector. Although necessary given the nature of the crisis, such measures have significant implications for
fiscal space. There is also considerable variation across individual countries. The reader is invited to look up details on policy
implementation for any country or country group using the “Assessment of Fiscal Policy Responses to COVID-19 Dashboard.”




14   >>>   A REVIEW OF FISCAL POLICY RESPONSES TO COVID-19
